content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 10, 2017

Andrew Morris
Town Attorney
Town of Mammoth Lakes
P O Box 1609
Mammoth Lakes, CA 93546

Re:     Your Request for Advice
         Our File No. A-17-021

Dear Mr. Morris:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Town of Mammoth Lakes Councilmember Cleland Hoff regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090. Please also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate.

QUESTION

Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Hoff from taking part in governmental decisions relating to the proposed development of the western portion of Mammoth Creek Park, including the potential construction of a "multi-use facility," given that the Councilmember leases her residence located within 500 feet of the Park?

CONCLUSION

No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions because they will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her real-property interest in the leasehold.

_________________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-021
Page No. 2

FACTS

Councilmember Hoff and her husband lease their residence, a condominium located within
500 feet of the western portion of Mammoth Creek Park (the "Park"), from a family trust (the
"Trust") formed by the Councilmember's husband's parents. The Councilmember's husband is a
beneficiary of the Trust because he stands to inherit a 50-percent ownership interest in the Trust's
real property. However, the Councilmember's husband does not presently receive income from the
Trust, and does not have an irrevocable future right to receive Trust income or principal.

The Town Council will soon consider whether to approve the proposed development of the
Park, including the potential construction of a "multi-use facility" (MUF), which would consist of
an ice rink and related equipment and possibly a community center either initially or in a later phase
of the project. The construction of the MUF would include a roofed structure and the installation of
chiller equipment. The development of the Park would also include parking improvements, lighting,
and a partial relocation of the Park's existing playground.

Residents of the neighborhood surrounding the Park have expressed concern that the
proposed development of the Park, in particular the potential construction and operation of the
MUF, will result in additional noise and light. The current plans for the development of the Park
include the construction of parking improvements within 500 feet of the Councilmember's
residence. However, it is not anticipated that the MUF will be visible from her residence. The
Councilmember notes that she does not believe that any decisions concerning the MUF will have
any effect on the value of her residence or her use and enjoyment of it.

You are the Town Attorney and the authorized representative of the Councilmember. You
provided additional facts on Thursday, March 2, 2017.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in the making, or
otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that an official has a "financial interest" in a
decision, within the meaning of the Act, if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on one or more of the official's interests identified in that section.

Under Section 87103(b), an official has an interest in any real property in which he or she
has a direct or indirect interest worth $2,000 or more. And Section 82033 defines an "interest in real
property" to include any leasehold worth $2,000 or more. Therefore, assuming the
Councilmember's leasehold is worth $2,000 or more, the Councilmember has a real-property
interest in the leasehold.²

We first examine whether the Councilmember also has a separate real-property interest in
her residence as a result of her husband being a beneficiary of the Trust that owns and leases the
real property to the Councilmember and her husband.

² "The value of a leasehold interest is the amount of rent owed during a 12-month period." (Regulation
18729(b).)

File No. A-17-021
Page No. 3

"Interests in real property of an individual includes a pro rata share of interests in real property of any business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater." (Section 82033.) "'Immediate family' means the spouse and dependent children." (Section 82029.)

Relevant to the facts here, Regulation 18234(c)(2) provides that, for purposes of determining whether an official has an interest in a trust's real property, the official has a direct, indirect, or beneficial interest in a trust if the official is a beneficiary of the trust, and (A) presently receives income from the trust, or (B) has an irrevocable future right to receive trust income or principal.

The Councilmember's husband stands to inherit a 50-percent interest in the Trust, and the Trust owns and leases the real property to the Councilmember and her husband. Although the Councilmember's husband is a beneficiary of the Trust, he does not receive income from the Trust, and does not have an irrevocable future right to receive trust income or principal. Therefore, the Councilmember does not have a direct, indirect, or beneficial interest in the Trust under Regulation 18234(c)(2). Accordingly, the Councilmember does not have a separate real-property interest in her residence as a result of her husband being a beneficiary of the Trust.

We now examine whether the decisions relating to the proposed development of the Park would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on the Councilmember's real-property interest in the leasehold.

Foreseeability and Materiality

The Councilmember's real-property interest in the leasehold is not explicitly involved in decisions regarding the development of the Park.³ Therefore, those decisions' effect on her real-property interest in the leasehold is reasonably foreseeable if it can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical. (Regulation 18701(b).)

Regulation 18702.2(b) provides that a decision's effect on any real property in which an official has a leasehold interest is material if the decision will: (1) change the termination date of the lease; (2) increase or decrease the potential rental value of the property; (3) increase or decrease the rental value of the property, and the official has a right to sublease the property; (4) change the official's actual or legally allowable use of the real property; or (5) impact the official's use and enjoyment of the real property.

None of the criteria set forth in Regulation 18702.2(b) is satisfied under the facts presented. There is no indication that the decisions at issue will affect the lease's termination date or the Councilmember's legally allowable use of the real property. Although some neighboring residents have expressed concern that the development of the Park, in particular the potential construction and operation of the MUF, will result in increased noise and light, it is not anticipated that the MUF will be visible from the Councilmember's residence. Furthermore, there is no indication that those concerns, if realized, would be sufficient to increase or decrease the rental value or potential rental

³ An interest is explicitly involved if it is a named party in, or the subject of, the governmental decision. If an interest is explicitly involved the effect of the decision is presumed foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(a).)

File No. A-17-021
Page No. 4

value of the Councilmember's residence, or to impact her use and enjoyment of it, regardless of
whether she has the right to sublease it. Thus, the decisions at issue will not have a material
financial effect on the Councilmember's real-property interest in the leasehold.

Accordingly, we conclude that the Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking
part in decisions relating to the proposed development of the western portion of the Park, including
the potential construction of the MUF.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By: Matthew F. Christy
Counsel, Legal Division

MFC:jgl

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Hoff from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to the proposed development of the western portion of Mammoth Creek Park, including the 
potential construction of a "multi-use facility," given that the Councilmember leases her residence 
located within 500 feet of the Park?
sections.conclusion:
No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions 
because they will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her real-property 
interest in the leasehold.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Hoff from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to the proposed development of the western portion of Mammoth Creek Park, including the 
potential construction of a "multi-use facility," given that the Councilmember leases her residence 
located within 500 feet of the Park?

CONCLUSION: No. The Act does not prohibit the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions 
because they will not have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on her real-property 
interest in the leasehold.
